candidate_problems.text: Does emergent branching plus redundant record formation explain only effective classicality, or can it genuinely account for single-outcome actuality? The conjecture narrows the problem but leaves unresolved whether 'one experienced branch' requires extra ontology beyond decoherence and self-stabilizing records.

What precise structural criteria make a branch a stable attractor of the interaction network? The conjecture relies on amplification, feedback, redundancy, and irreversibility, but it remains open how to define and derive these conditions rigorously enough to predict when definiteness emerges and where the quantum-classical boundary effectively lies.

How, if at all, do amplitude weights generate the observed Born-rule probabilities within this system-level picture? The conjecture says possible stable branches are weighted by amplitudes, but it does not explain why experienced frequencies or credences should match squared amplitudes rather than some other branch-measure.
